concepts.name: Incentives

card.question: Freakonomics groups incentives into which three types?

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Freakonomics' core claim: people respond to **incentives**, and schemes fail when
designers misread them.

## The three types

- **Economic** — money/material gain.
- **Social** — reputation, how others see you.
- **Moral** — conscience, right vs wrong.

Most behaviour mixes all three; targeting only one often backfires.

## Example

A fine for late daycare pickup (economic) **crowded out** the moral incentive and
*increased* lateness — parents now "bought" the right to be late.

## Gotchas

- Strong incentives invite **gaming** — people optimize the metric, not the goal.

concepts.name: Opportunity Cost

card.question: The opportunity cost of a choice is:
card.answer: The value of the next-best alternative forgone
distractors: The total value of all alternatives forgone

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Every choice forecloses others. **Opportunity cost** is how economists measure the
true cost of a decision.

## Definition

Opportunity cost = the value of the **single next-best alternative** you give up —
not the sum of all alternatives, and not just the cash outlay.

## Example

Spending an evening studying "costs" the shift you didn't work (say \$60) — that
forgone wage is the opportunity cost, even though studying is "free".

## Gotchas

- Include **implicit** costs (your time), not just money spent.
- Sunk costs are **not** opportunity costs — they're already gone.

concepts.name: Thinking at the Margin

card.question: Thinking at the margin means deciding based on:
card.answer: The benefit and cost of one more unit

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Good decisions happen **at the margin** — about the next unit, not the whole.

## Definition

Compare the **marginal benefit** and **marginal cost** of one more unit (hour, item,
mile). Keep doing the activity while MB > MC; the optimum is where **MB = MC**.

## Example

Studying hour 1 raises your grade a lot (high MB); hour 6 barely helps (low MB). Stop
when the next hour's benefit no longer beats its cost (sleep, time).

## Gotchas

- Ignore **sunk** and **total** amounts — only the *next* unit's costs/benefits
  count.

concepts.name: Correlation vs Causation

card.question: A spurious correlation between A and B is best explained by:
card.answer: A confounding variable that drives both

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Data shows things moving together; jumping to "A causes B" is the classic error.

## Definition

- **Correlation:** two variables move together.
- **Causation:** one actually drives the other.
- A **spurious correlation** is produced by a hidden **confounder** affecting both.

## Example

Ice-cream sales and drownings rise together — both are driven by **hot weather**
(the confounder), not by each other.

## Gotchas

- "Correlation ≠ causation." To claim causation you need an experiment or a credible
  natural experiment, not just association.

concepts.name: Moral Hazard

card.question: Moral hazard arises from:
card.answer: Hidden action after a deal, when a party doesn't bear the full risk

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Insurance and contracts change behaviour. **Moral hazard** is the risk created
*after* a deal.

## Definition

**Moral hazard:** after an agreement, a party takes **more risk** because they don't
bear the full consequences — a hidden **action**.

## Example

A fully-insured driver drives less carefully; the insurer absorbs the loss. The
behaviour change happens **post-agreement**.

## Gotchas

- Contrast **adverse selection** (hidden *information*, **before** the deal). Moral
  hazard = hidden *action*, **after**.

concepts.name: Adverse Selection

card.question: Adverse selection differs from moral hazard in that it involves:
card.answer: Hidden information before the deal (who chooses to participate)

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

When one side knows more **before** a deal, the pool of participants skews badly.

## Definition

**Adverse selection:** hidden **information** at the time of contracting means the
riskiest types are the keenest to participate.

## Example

The sickest people buy the most health insurance (they know their risk); the insurer
can't tell them apart, so prices rise and the healthy drop out.

## Gotchas

- Before-the-deal hidden *info* = adverse selection; after-the-deal hidden *action*
  = moral hazard. Fixes: signalling, screening, mandates.

concepts.name: Information Asymmetry

card.question: Information asymmetry lets an expert (e.g. an agent):
card.answer: Exploit knowing more than the client to serve their own incentives
distractors: Always act in the client's best interest

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Markets assume informed parties. When one side **knows more**, it can exploit the
other — the root of adverse selection and moral hazard.

## Definition

**Information asymmetry:** one party (mechanic, realtor, doctor, agent) has more
relevant knowledge than the other and can act in their own interest.

## Example

A mechanic recommends an unnecessary repair; the customer can't verify it. The
expert's information advantage becomes a conflict of interest.

## Gotchas

- Remedies reduce the gap: reputation, warranties, third-party certification,
  aligned incentives.

concepts.name: Unintended Consequences

card.question: The daycare late-pickup fine increased lateness because it:
card.answer: Reframed a moral breach as a small price worth paying
distractors: Was priced too high for parents to afford
Made parents arrive earlier on average

concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Incentives ripple. Policies often produce effects **nobody intended** — a recurring
Freakonomics theme.

## Definition

**Unintended consequences:** an intervention changes behaviour in ways that
undercut (or reverse) its goal.

## Example

The daycare late-pickup **fine** reframed a moral obligation as a cheap price, so
lateness **rose** — the opposite of the intent.

## Gotchas

- Ask "how will people **re-optimize**?" before deploying an incentive; prices can
  crowd out morality.

concepts.name: Decision Math (Opportunity Cost, Margin, EV)
concepts.theory_md:
## Why this exists

Economics is decision-making under scarcity. This drills the **quantitative tools**
behind good choices.

## The tools

- **Opportunity cost** — value of the next-best alternative given up. The real cost
  of a choice, not the cash price.
- **Marginal net benefit** — benefit of *one more* unit minus its cost; keep going
  while it's positive (optimum where marginal benefit = marginal cost).
- **Expected value** — probability-weighted average payoff
  $E = \sum p_i x_i$; the rational criterion under risk.
- **Percent change** — $\dfrac{\text{new}-\text{old}}{\text{old}}\times100\%$.
- **ROI** — return on investment $=\dfrac{\text{gain}-\text{cost}}{\text{cost}}$.
- **Elasticity** — % change in quantity ÷ % change in price; $|E|>1$ elastic,
  $<1$ inelastic.

## Worked example

A \$100 bet pays \$300 with probability 0.4: $E = 0.4(300)+0.6(0)=120 > 100$ ⇒
positive expected value.

## Gotchas

- Opportunity cost counts the **forgone** option, not all alternatives.
- Marginal thinking ignores **sunk** costs — only the next unit matters.
